_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Presence of symptoms such as palpable abdominal mass, abdominal pain, fever, fatigue, vomiting, weight loss, hematuria, recurrent urinary infections, or a soft, slowly growing and painless retroperitoneal cystic mass.
Definitive diagnosis of cystic lymphangioma achieved by histological examination subsequent to surgery or exploratory laparotomy.

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure?
USG/CT/MRI Abdomen (show insinuating nature crossing multiple compartments)

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge available?
Did the histopathological report confirm the diagnosis of retroperitoneal lymphangioma?

common_queries: Clinical notes missing or lacking detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure at pre-authorization.
Clinical photograph not provided at pre-authorization.
USG/CT/MRI Abdomen report missing or not demonstrating insinuating nature crossing multiple compartments at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and line of treatment not available at claim submission.
Detailed Procedure / Operative Notes not available at claim submission.
Discharge summary with follow-up advice not available at claim submission.
Histopathological report did not confirm the diagnosis of retroperitoneal lymphangioma at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Lymphangiomas are relatively- rare and benign lesions believed to be congenital malformations of the lymphatic anlage rather than true tumors.
Retroperitoneal lymphangiomas are also relatively rare and account for 1% to 10% of all lymphangiomas.
Most retroperitoneal lymphangiomas typically occur in children under 2 years old; however, in some cases, these lesions can be found after a long asymptomatic period due to slow development.
Three histological types of lymphangiomas are described: cystic, capillary and cavernous.
Retroperitoneal lymphangioma is commonly of cystic type.
Most cystic lymphangiomas in the body are present in the first 2 years of life, however lymphangiomas of the retroperitoneum are usually diagnosed in older children or adults.
surgical_options: The optimum treatment of cystic lymphangioma is complete resection.
